feedback.text: I strongly support this Act, the abuses of massive corporations in the digital sector has gotten out of hand. They absolutely need to regulated and reminded that they are not all powerful nor are they above the law. They have been abusing their overwhelming legal budget to bully consumers for too long. Massive corporations need to be held to a much stricter standard of fair practise if not outright legally handicapped to compensate for the unfair advantage they have bought though immense legal teams and extremely well funded lobbing efforts. I have two main points of feed back for the act, both additions of things that I believe should added to the act in the interest of fairness. Firstly protections form abusive licencing and purchase practises from video game publishers, these publishers have been falsely advertising the purchase of arbitrarily revocable licences for games as the actual purchase of fully owned copies/instances of the games. The arbitrary revocation of these licences en masse is widely referred to as "killing" the game. There have been massively supported initiatives such as Stop Killing Games that are looking to stop this practise, which I think could be a good fit for this act as it is based on opposing the unfair practises of publishers denying access to purchased games and trying to prevent preservation efforts of actual artistic works. Secondly far more restrictions need to be imposed on the insane End User Licance Agreements (ELUA) and Terms Of Service (TOS) agreements that these companies have been forcing on customers. Length and complexity restrictions need to be put in place. These agreements have become, enormous, unwieldy, jargon filled monoliths of unfair, excessive and frankly insane terms and conditions. All of which are hidden within bloated and redundant legal language with the obvious intent to be too long and too awkward for users to reasonably digest especially when these massive legal contracts are slammed onto users right before they are given access to the content they came for, and AFTER they have already paid for the content in many cases. This means that users are effectively forced to unknowingly and uninformedly accept the corporations every whim and they know it. Emboldening them to make more and more intrusive and exploitative conditions. Demanding access to massive amounts of personal and sensitive information from the device (arbitrary file access, access to memory of other running programs, access to the list of other running programs, access to browsing activity and history, full name, age, email address, home address, phone number and more), demanding unnecessary security access to the device (admin privileges, kernel access, etc) not to mention the insane legal terms, binding individual clauses, class action waivers, jury trial waivers, all of which are solely in place to shield the corporations from the legal and reputational consequences of any corporate wrong doing. As if that wasn't enough publishers of creative tools or other programs that have secondary creative features have started to demand total control and access to the IP rights to all works (and/or User Generated Content UGC) created with their programs, including to be used as training data for generative AI models, with 0 compensation to creators and no express permission or consent, just the overreaching and mandatory ELUA/TOS. Users should not be expected and absolutely should not be REQUIRED to sign their souls away to these corporations the way the current market standard ELUA/TOS demand, just to use software available for public "purchase" and/or paid/unpaid use. The publishers have shown that they are unable to self-regulate when it comes to these agreements, and due to the lack of bargaining power of consumers, these unfair terms should not be legally binding to consumers while agreements made by the companies to consumers on the other hand should be fully binding. the balance of power must shift.
